subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree in Molecular or Cellular Biology
Completed undergraduate studies in a biology-related field before pursuing advanced degrees
Received additional specialized training in molecular biology techniques such as PCR and Western blotting
Attended professional workshops and seminars to stay updated on lab methodologies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Lab Director for a molecular and microbiology laboratory
Responsible for overseeing undergraduate and graduate student research projects
Directly involved in experimental design, troubleshooting, and mentoring students
Ensures smooth laboratory operations and compliance with protocols

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the academic research sector
Specializes in life sciences, specifically molecular and cellular biology
Collaborates within a university or higher education institution environment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works in a medium-sized academic laboratory
Lab typically comprises 10-20 people including faculty, graduate, and undergraduate students
Collaborative, educational environment focused on research and learning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Academic research laboratory within a university or college
Focuses on basic and applied research in molecular and microbiology
Supports both teaching and research missions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has more than 10 years of experience in molecular and cellular biology
Served in a lab leadership or director role for several years
Has mentored multiple cohorts of undergraduate and graduate students over the past decade

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds a senior-level position as a lab director
Considered an expert in molecular and cellular biology techniques
Experience includes both bench science and supervisory responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Guides and supervises undergraduate and graduate students in experimental work
Designs, plans, and troubleshoots molecular biology experiments (e.g., PCR, Western blot)
Conducts hands-on bench experiments as needed, especially for time-sensitive tasks
Reviews and verifies experimental protocols and calculations with students

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Time at the bench varies daily, sometimes significant, sometimes minimal
A substantial portion of time is devoted to troubleshooting experiments
At certain times of the year, more time is spent supporting undergraduates with their projects
Administrative and supervisory duties balance out with hands-on laboratory work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates with a high degree of autonomy in both scientific and managerial decisions
Has authority to design experiments and approve protocols
Decides on allocation of resources and task assignments within the lab
Mentors students in decision-making while retaining final oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Mentoring students and explaining core molecular biology concepts
Designing standard PCR experiments and setting up master mixes
Identifying common experimental errors and offering quick troubleshooting steps

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Troubleshooting experiments with ambiguous or inconsistent results
Managing time-sensitive or multi-step experiments under tight deadlines
Balancing the diverse skill levels of undergraduate and graduate students during collaborative projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Preparing and calculating reagent dilutions for multiple PCR setups
Recording and organizing experimental results into spreadsheets or lab notebooks
Routine background research on genes or protocols prior to experiments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Finds experimental troubleshooting and successful mentoring highly rewarding
Enjoys collaborative problem-solving sessions with students
Considers repetitive calculations and data entry less engaging
Tedious tasks include updating lab inventories and reagent logs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Frequent need to re-explain basic experimental concepts to new students
Time lost to manual calculations and data transcription
Occasional miscommunication or errors due to inconsistent protocol adherence

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher productivity and enjoyment when working on complex experiments and troubleshooting
Skill level allows for efficient multitasking and rapid identification of errors
Less enjoyment during repetitive or administrative tasks, which can slow overall productivity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open and curious about adopting new technologies that improve workflow
Encourages students to experiment with new tools, including AI platforms
Believes in staying current with technological advancements in the field

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new software, particularly when it has clear relevance to lab work
Comfortable with trying emerging AI tools and integrating them into existing workflows
Often leads by example in exploring new digital resources

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate time to learning tools that offer clear benefits
Participates in workshops or webinars on technological advancements
Supports students in their own tech learning journeys

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential for increased efficiency and accuracy in experiments
Workload can sometimes limit time available for in-depth tech training
Relevance to daily tasks is a primary factor in prioritizing which tools to learn

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers and students’ enthusiasm for new tools positively influences adoption
Lab culture encourages knowledge sharing about new software and tech
Positive experiences from colleagues validate investing time in tool adoption

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Google for literature searches and background research
Employs ChatGPT and similar LLMs for calculations, troubleshooting, and background queries
Relies on digital lab manuals and protocol databases

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Uses PCR machines, gel electrophoresis equipment, and Western blot apparatus
Maintains physical and electronic lab notebooks for record-keeping
References printed and digital lab protocols regularly

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Highly familiar with standard molecular biology instrumentation and techniques
Proficient with commonly used lab management and data recording systems
Stays informed about new lab technologies through professional development

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces difficulty transferring data between digital notebooks and analysis software
Some AI tools lack integration with proprietary lab management systems
Manual steps are often needed to bridge gaps between calculation tools and experimental documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts of large language models and their capabilities
Knowledgeable about the distinction between generative AI and traditional automation
Regularly discusses AI use cases with students and colleagues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT for calculations, troubleshooting, and background research
Students occasionally use other AI chatbots or calculators for experiment planning
Faculty colleagues also use ChatGPT for writing test questions and summaries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: AI tools are used several times a week for experiment-related queries
Commonly applies AI for calculating reagent dilutions and master mixes
Uses AI to generate background on target genes and expected product sizes
AI is consulted for troubleshooting unexpected experimental results

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI successfully helps with quick calculations and protocol clarifications
On occasion, AI-generated suggestions require manual verification due to potential inaccuracies
Learned to always cross-check AI outputs with trusted sources, especially for critical experiments
Students have sometimes relied too heavily on AI, underscoring the need for domain expertise

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal organizational AI training programs in place yet
Peers and students frequently share tips and best practices informally
Adoption of AI is primarily self-directed, supported by community knowledge sharing

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI, especially when troubleshooting
Frequently interacts with AI in tandem with student input for educational value
Occasionally uses AI independently for quick checks or confirmations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Treats AI as an assistant or advisor during experimental planning
Encourages students to view AI as a supplementary resource, not a replacement for critical thinking
Utilizes AI as a co-worker for repetitive calculation tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to piloting AI-driven workflow enhancements
Encourages the lab team to explore new AI features as they become available
Believes AI-driven processes will become increasingly integral to lab work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for flexibility and ease of use
Finds chat-based AI interactions more accessible for students and faculty
Values the ability to refine requests in real-time based on AI responses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many laboratory tasks require significant tacit domain knowledge
AI is primarily used for routine or well-defined aspects, not nuanced experimental design
Human oversight remains essential for interpreting complex or unexpected results

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Moderate trust in AI for straightforward calculations and protocol lookups
Does not fully trust AI for critical or safety-sensitive decisions without human verification
Checks AI recommendations against established protocols and literature

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive or computationally intensive tasks
Believes human expertise should guide experimental design and interpretation
Supports a collaborative approach where AI augments but does not replace human judgment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable for routine calculations and data management
Finds it threatening if AI is used for tasks requiring contextual, domain-specific insight
Advocates for human review of all critical AI-generated outputs

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: AI enhances the importance of critical thinking and experimental design skills
Some routine technical skills may be deprioritized as AI improves
Mentorship and interpretation skills remain highly valued

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Requires proficiency in evaluating and validating AI-generated outputs
Emergence of digital literacy as a core skill for students and faculty
Responsibility for teaching responsible AI use and verification techniques

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Low concern about AI replacing core mentoring or experimental design roles
Some awareness that repetitive calculation and data entry tasks may become automated
Overall role seen as secure due to reliance on human expertise

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manual review and cross-verification are standard practices for AI-supported tasks
Lab requires human sign-off on critical calculations and experimental plans
Override mechanisms include reverting to traditional calculation methods when needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: AI adoption is becoming normalized across the lab and broader institution
Policies are evolving to encourage responsible AI use and verification
No formal mandates yet, but increasing expectation to leverage AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Views AI as an increasingly integral part of academic and research workflows
Believes AI will drive greater efficiency and accessibility in science
Acknowledges potential for both positive and disruptive industry-wide changes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes ethical AI use requires transparency and human oversight
Worries about over-reliance on AI leading to loss of foundational skills
Advocates for responsible use, with proper cross-checking and data privacy safeguards

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires deeper integration between AI tools and lab management systems
Would benefit from AI that can auto-document experiments and generate standardized reports
Sees value in AI assistants capable of real-time protocol verification and alerting to potential errors

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates teaching AI literacy as a regular part of student mentoring
Expects AI to handle more routine lab tasks, freeing time for complex research and mentoring
Believes core scientific skills will remain essential for interpreting AI outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Recommends formal training programs for faculty, staff, and students on AI tool use
Supports development of guidelines for responsible AI adoption and verification
Advocates for investment in AI-lab integration to maximize productivity and data integrity
